Full Length Article
Seizing the means of innovation: On the relationship between Marxism and ecomodernism

, 
10.1016/j.polgeo.2025.103388
Seizing the means of innovation: On the relationship between Marxism and ecomodernism

Outline

  1. Abstract
  2. 1. Introduction
  3. 2. Ecomodernism – rapture not restoration
  4. 3. Marxist weltanschauung and technological innovation
  5. 4. A Marxist critique of mainstream ecomodernism
  6. 5. Toward Marxist axioms of technology and innovation
  7. 6. Afterthought
  8. CRediT authorship contribution statement
  9. Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
  10. Declaration of competing interest
  11. Acknowledgements
  12. References

Abstract

Recent efforts to articulate a “socialist ecomodernist” politics have spurred debate over the relationship between Marxism and ecomodernism. Degrowth-aligned socialists critique ecomodernism for its productivism and naive techno-optimism; ecomodernist socialists respond that ecomodernism's grounding of ecological politics in human material needs and focus on production's technological metabolism broadly aligns with historical materialism. This paper first outlines the homologies and contradictions between Marxism and ecomodernism and then turns to one area of potential dialectical synthesis: addressing capitalism's ecological crises requires a systematic account of technological innovation. To this end we put forward eleven axioms distilling Marx's philosophico-anthropological account of technology. These axioms reflect Marx's understanding of technological innovation as both a source of tension, since productive forces consistently outpace the social world and the relations of production, and crucial to transcending class societies. We argue that a Marxist response to climate breakdown must address how a communist movement will manage the unintended, indirect ecological impacts of production. Marxists have always understood that the proletariat must control the means of production in order to address class-based inequality. In the era of climate breakdown, Marxists must be equally explicit about technological innovation. If we are to protect nature while progressing toward Marx's “realm of freedom”, the task now is also to seize and reconfigure the means of innovation.
  • Previous article in this issue
  • Next article in this issue

1. Introduction

Marxist scholars have offered a compelling diagnosis of the underlying causes of climate breakdown – locating the crisis in the conjunction of capitalist relations of production, structures of accumulation and extraction, and the associated ensemble of state power and ideologies (Moore, 2017). The Anthropocene's compounding disasters – fire, flood, famine, ethno-nationalism, imperial warfare, displacement, and poverty – bear out this critique. Yet diagnosis is not cure. Nearly everywhere, left political parties are marginalised. Workers' movements are either ecologically dislocated or only sporadically engaged with the work of healing capitalism's ecological wounds. What feasible program does the radical left offer to avert overshoot beyond 1.5 °C or 2 °C? Degrowth advocates promise reduce material throughput and reimagine prosperity (Kallis, 2019; Gómez-Baggethun, 2020; Hickel et al., 2022; Saito, 2023), while socialist ecomodernists advocate universal energy access and transformation of the productive forces and their “concrete class” relations (Phillips, 2015; Huber, 2019; 2021 & 2023; Huber & Phillips, 2024). They argue these relations of production will ultimately dictate the relationship between human society and the planet (Huber, 2022a). A third group explores the potential for technological innovation to promote just social-ecological change in ways that seek to synthesise the two approaches (Lawhon et al., 2021; Luque-Lora, 2021; Paulson, 2021, 2024; Robbins, 2020).
In this emerging debate between socialist degrowth and ecomodernist positions, both sides agree that economic activity must serve human needs rather than capital accumulation – use-value over exchange-value. However, they disagree over whether our response to ecological crises should focus more on the scale or the technological composition of the “productive forces” – Marx's term for the combination of a society's scientific and social knowledge, technological capacity, and the form, ability and division of labour. Advocates of each position have their own exegetical and hermeneutical approaches and have found textual support in Marx's own writing (see Robbins, 2020; Saito, 2023; Huber & Phillips, 2024). Each side has given birth to its own unique Marx. Such an intellectual exercise is constructive – the Marxist tradition contains invaluable resources for emancipatory political change at any time. However, a historical materialist response to the climate crisis requires fresh analysis that builds on Marx and Engels' oeuvre without being limited to exegesis. After all, there was no parallel to the climate crisis in the nineteenth century. Marx could only address the concrete ecological challenges of his era (e.g. soil fertility). More recently, earth system sciences have identified a second set of indirect, unintended consequences of production (e.g. ocean acidification, ozone depletion, climate change). How should historical materialist analysis incorporate this new understanding that industrial activity has a second loop of excess extraneous impacts? Our goal in this paper is both to critically examine the relationship between Marxism and ecomodernism and to advance Marxist thinking on the role of technology in ecological restoration.
While we endorse Huber's claim that 'productive forces' constitute the 'fulcrum of any new relation to the planet' (Huber, 2022a), we advance scholarship by: first, formulating a Marxist analysis of ecomodernism that acknowledges both areas of alignment and contradiction with socialism; second, developing an account of technological innovation'splace within Marxist tradition. We use 'innovation' as shorthand for advances in socio-technological and scientific elements of productive forces, specifically those meeting human needs without greenhouse gas emissions. Our historical assessment of Marxist movements since the Bolshevik Revolution indicates that these movements only develop innovation as a political paradigm when they control both national production and state apparatuses. Western Marxists, on account of their distinct philosophical and class trajectory and detachment from concrete material power have given less attention to innovation. However, we argue that responding to ecological crises will require the Marxist tradition to develop a more systematic materialist theorisation of technology.
Our argument develops in four parts. First, we examine mainstream ecomodernism, whose contribution lies in identifying 'mission-oriented' innovation – decarbonising society's technological metabolism – as foundational to any climate response, though it falters by assuming implementation through existing state-capitalist institutions. Second, we recapitulate Marxism's epistemological tools, methodological equation and Weltanschauung(“world view” or “philosophy of life”). Third, we contrast ecomodernist and Marxist accounts of technology, first elaborating a Marxist critique of ecomodernism, and then conclude by theorising how the Marxist socio-technological equation should synthesise ecomodernist insights (part four). Here, we address the empirical under-theorisation of technology and innovation within contemporary Marxism (Roos & Hornborg, 2024) by distilling eleven axioms that present a holistic Marxist conception of technology.
Marxists have always understood that addressing exploitation, expropriation and class-based inequality requires that the proletariat own and control the means of production and socio-political institutions responsible for decision-making. In the era of climate breakdown, addressing exploitation of nature alongside rectifying geopolitical inequalities requires a shift in emphasis: either Marxism must shed its productivism and embrace radical degrowth (Foster, 2016; Saito, 2023) or it must develop its account of technological transformation. In this case, one of the proletariat's most immediate climate-linked tasks, after revolting against capital and the state, must be seizing and reconfiguring the means of innovating and deploying clean technologies. The concept that ecomodernists sometimes call “mission-oriented innovation” (see Mazzucato, 2018) describes one essential element of a necessary transformation in scientific knowledge production and social metabolism. Only by liberating the productive forces from the existing regime of private property and transforming them according to the twin imperatives of the material needs of humanity, and ecological health can we expect transition from the “realm of necessity” to Marx's “realm of freedom”.

2. Ecomodernism – rapture not restoration

Ecomodernism is not a self-contained social theory or comprehensive ideology. Instead, it might best be understood as an intervention within Western environmentalism concerning technology's potential contribution to ecological challenges. Primarily associated with the California-based Breakthrough Institute and WePlanet NGO, ecomodernism has a patchy reputation on account of its rejection of green technological preferences, and its defence of growth. Nevertheless, one sympathetic account holds that ecomodernism's most distinct contribution is its defense of a broadly social democratic response to climate change against both “green” and “neoliberal” positions that reject the role of either technological innovation or industry policy (Symons, 2019).
Ecomodernists reject the mantra of “harmonizing with nature”, advocating instead for absolute decoupling through production intensification and reduced human-nature entanglement (Asafu-Adjaye et al., 2015). As against the green preference for small-scale and traditional technologies, ecomodernists argue that societies should carefully apply their full social and technological powers to addressing ecological challenges; against liberal economists' belief that well-managed markets can resolve ecological challenges, ecomodernists argue that only some form of collective public investment in developing and deploying new technologies can eliminate the negative externalities of production; against green arguments that the South must be protected from hubristic technologies (e.g., fertiliser and fossil fuel use, nuclear power, genetic engineering), ecomodernists defend Southern self-determination, even including expanded access to fossil fuels as a tool of poverty alleviation; finally, against the degrowth movement's claim that a good society should consume less energy (Hickel et al., 2022), ecomodernists argue that deploying abundant zero-carbon energy (in some accounts as a ‘public utility’, Nordhaus, 2019) is the key to enabling simultaneous human and ecological flourishing.
A comparison of degrowth and ecomodernist geographical imaginaries may be instructive. The degrowth movement, with its goals of equitable “degrowth of material and energy consumption” (Kallis, 2019, p. 192) seeks to re-embed the “social metabolism” and restore roughly its pre-capitalist relationship with the “universal metabolism” of nature. They would limit additional impacts on ecosystem dynamics by developing innovative forms of social provisioning capable of meeting the needs of 8 billion people with limited energy or material throughput. Socialist-ecomodernists critique the concept of a “metabolic rift” for contradicting both science and Marx's dialectical materialism. They argue capitalism's resource misallocation creates ecological threats that can be comprehended without recourse to an unscientific belief in a fragile balance of nature or the rigid typological division between the two spheres (Huber & Phillips, 2024). By contrast, ecomodernism's teleology resembles Christian 'Rapture' – detaching human communities from earthly ecosystem dependence. In this vision abundant clean energy – most likely generated via nuclear fusion or fission – is fungible for ecosystem services. Expressed in terms of Foster's metabolic theory, Ecomodernists are seeking to detach the “social metabolism” from predation on the “universal metabolism” of nature (Foster, 2016). With abundant energy and advanced technologies (e.g. precision fermentation replacing animal agriculture), food and fuel would increasingly be synthesized without land-intensive farming or energy extraction (Karlsson, 2020). Circular production processes will reduce the need for mineral extraction, while dense urban forms will minimize the land requirements of human settlement.
Why is ecomodernism widely reviled as ‘reactionary’ (Heron, 2024) when its core claims align with rationalist progressive thought, including aspects of Marxism? We suggest this puzzle is easily explained: on the one hand ecomodernists have deliberately antagonised the technophobic, romantic-atavistic ecologism of the wider green left by means of their contrarian productivism and support for specific controversial technologies such as nuclear power; on the other hand, most Marxists have rejected mainstream ecomodernism as it lacks sustained critique of capitalist relations of production, historical systems of hierarchy, repressive state-civil society relations, or the existing social configuration of class and imperial relations.
Ecomodernism emerged from within the American environmental movement as a critique of that movement's response to climate change. The Breakthrough Institute's founders alleged that since the environmental movement had often failed to include “humans and economic development” within their “conception of the environment” they had failed “to articulate an inspiring and positive vision” (Shellenberger & Nordhaus, 2009, pp. 121, 133). Ecomodernists have repeatedly sought to shed the climate movement's apocalyptic rhetoric and to conjure a vision of a “good” or “charming Anthropocene” in which the wise application of techno-engineering capacities and, in some accounts, deliberate social processes of re-enchantment (Buck, 2015) allow universal human development to occur alongside ecological “flourishing.” Ironically, ecomodernism could equally be critiqued for lacking a positive vision: it advocates for avoidance of ecological crisis alongside a status-quo vision of continued modernisation and socio-technological change within a capitalist-imperialist system.
The Ecomodernist Manifesto defines modernisation as the “long-term evolution of social, economic, political, and technological arrangements in human societies toward vastly improved material well-being, public health, resource productivity, economic integration, shared infrastructure, and personal freedom” which it insists are distinct from “capitalism, corporate power, and laissez-faire economic policies”. It argues that “by understanding and promoting these emergent processes” of modernisation, “humans have the opportunity to re-wild and re-green the Earth – even as developing countries achieve modern living standards, and material poverty ends” (Asafu-Adjaye et al., 2015, p. 26). The Manifesto's uncritical celebration of modernisation, and its implicit acceptance of capitalist modernity, colonial legacies and neo-imperialism, suggests that its authors view continuation of existing trends, absent ecological destruction – as a sufficient goal:
Modernization has liberated ever more people from lives of poverty and hard agricultural labor, women from chattel status, children and ethnic minorities from oppression, and societies from capricious and arbitrary governance. Greater resource productivity associated with modern sociotechnological systems has allowed human societies to meet human needs with fewer resource inputs and less impact on the environment (Asafu-Adjaye et al., 2015, pp. 28-9).
Some ecomodernist writers have articulated a more developed social vision with a critique of existing capitalist-statist social formations and strategies of social mobilisation. Foremost here are those Marxist-ecomodernists who have placed organised labor movements and class conflict at the heart of climate analysis (Huber, 2022b; Huber & Phillips, 2024) or who have argued that structural equity should be prioritised ahead of an immediate transition away from fossil fuels in the South (Jayaraman, 2022). Mainstream ecomodernists have variously argued for progress toward universal equality made possible through broad social investments (Karlsson, 2020), that achievement of the Ecomodernist goal of universal human flourishing requires universal public provision of social services (Symons, 2019) or for a worker-led project of ecological restoration (Buck, 2018; Parenti, 2020). However, while a politics that grows out of bourgeois Western environmentalism and its “professional managerial class” might usefully challenge some destructive elements of bourgeois reasoning, and even connect with strands of Third World Marxism (Jayaraman, 2022), it does not articulate a response to the crisis confronting people in the lower echelons of the global division of labor. Ecomodernism's strengths and limitations are perhaps illustrated by the boast that “the Breakthrough Institute's long-standing advocacy of technology and investment-led climate innovation policy” was “validated” by the Inflation Reduction Act (Nordhaus & Trembath, 2023). “Bidenomics”, although departing from neoliberalism, is still an exercise of imperial state power that advances the interests of American capital and workers at the expense of workers the world over (Harris, 2024).
For Marxists, ecomodernism lacks a materialist method of examining modalities of power or a program for overcoming these innate contradictions and antagonisms. It remains narrowly focused on technical policy-interventions that could eliminate specifically ecological roadblocks to human development. Ecomodernism lacks any broader critique of the systems of capitalist exploitation that make inequality and uneven development a constitutive element of contemporary global capitalism. By contrast, Marxism, especially its radical Third World iterations, aspires to transcend mere epistemological criticism. The Marxist praxeological equation unites reflection with a program of action within critique. Critique, as the unity of opposite forces, only gains potency when it becomes contagious among the working masses and dialectically indivisible from practice/labor itself. As Marx declares, “material force must be overthrown by material force; but theory also becomes a material force as soon as it has gripped the masses” (Marx, 1844, p. 7). Thus, while ecomodernism may yield valuable policy insights concerning innovation's role, Marxists anticipate ecomodernism can only become impactful if it is grounded in an understanding of praxis that presents a concrete critique of capitalist relations of production and mobilizes exploited classes.

3. Marxist weltanschauung and technological innovation

Outlining a Marxist conception of technology requires that we first specify our understanding of his system. What constitutes Marxist philosophy and method, let alone its conception of technology, is contentious (Harvey, 2019). Lenin's hypothesis was that Marx's system has concrete, often ideologically perverse, effects on bourgeois science, society and consciousness (Lenin, 1908 & 1913). Here he echoed Engels' earlier observation that European socialist movements were enfeebled by a “crowd of students, literati … other young déclassé bourgeois” who treat their bourgeois education and ideas as entitlement to truth and a revolutionary position (Marx & Engels, 1975, p. 21). The same allegation might be made today against Western lifestyle activists and dilettante bohemians who have subverted Marxism, disarmed its revolutionary system and demonized and undermined “Third World Marxism”, “real socialist” states, and revolutionary movements (see critique of localist politics in Srnicek and Williams, 2015).
What is Marxism then? Marxism is a dialectical analytical framework emerging from the radical strand of enlightenment that seeks to integrate advances from across the natural sciences into social analysis. Much like Darwin's theory of biological evolution revealed the mechanisms of species development, similarly Marx applied Hegel's inverted system within a philosophic-anthropological formula of natural-social evolution that outlines laws of development in a non-teleological history (Engels, 1925). Charles Lyell's geological insights, which had influenced Marx since his formative years in the 1830s and 1840s, informed Marx's view of deep anthropological historical time as gradual yet transformative (Woods & Grant, 1995). Delving deeper into political economy in the 1850s and 1860s, Marx became engrossed in Justus von Liebig's agricultural chemistry, which introduced "metabolic interaction" between humans and nature, shaping his understanding of how social formations relate to nature (capitalist agriculture and soil depletion) (see Huber & Phillips, 2024). Similarly, the work of Hermann von Helmholtz, James Prescott Joule and Rudolf Clausius on thermodynamics, energy and electromagnetism, which captured Marx's attention in the late 1850s, provided insights into the fundamental forces shaping physical reality, reinforcing Marx's view of society as an interconnected system of co-evolving forces (Bukharin, 1933). In the 1870s, Marx and Engels explored Lewis Henry Morgan's anthropological studies on ancient societies, which provided his scientific formula with an anthropological understanding of the evolution of social structures and property relations; this informed concepts like primitive communal systems sublated [aufheben] into class societies in various typologies (Engels, 2004, p. 54). This synthesis of natural and social sciences gave rise to an ontology and methodology that aimed to provide a comprehensive analysis of capitalist society and its potential for revolutionary change. In other words, Marx's theoretical framework was both destructive (a weapon aimed at “rotten bourgeois civilization”) and creative (a practice and guide to action that aimed to create a classless post-capitalist society).
Yet, enduring debates within Marxist theory reveal its ambiguity, underscoring the need for an open and creative hermeneutics. Perhaps an effective way to approach Marxism is through a set of foundational axioms, concepts and notions. This may allow us a creative hermeneutical leeway from repetitive exegetical approaches, one that allows us to start with and not end with Marx's work (Amin in Kvangraven, 2020). These foundational concepts include: dialectics, materialism, mode of production, surplus value, theory of history and technology, revolutionary critique, ideology and false-consciousness, and finally, totality.
Marxism rests upon the philosophical bedrock of dialectical materialism, a mode of Hegelian-influenced analysis that aspires to apprehend reality in its totality, motion, and contradiction. This method, as Mao points out, provides the epistemological-ontological and political tools to dissect the anatomy of social relations, formations and their historical development (Mao, 2019, pp. 8–13). Marx's materialist dialectics transcend the limitations of Aristotelian-Leibnizian formal logic's self-contained eternal judgements, propositions and syllogisms. Dialectical materialism posits three laws: quantity-quality transformation, interpenetration of opposites, and negation of negation–driving nature, society, social relations, and thought (Engels, 1951). Marxist scholars like Alen Wood argue that these laws, operating in friction, weave existence's disparate threads into a cohesive evolutionary whole. Understanding these laws is believed to reveal the concrete universal in its perpetual becoming (Wood, 1998).
Historical materialism posits the ontological primacy of objective reality over consciousness (Marx & Engels, 2000). It emerges as the dialectical negation of German idealism and sublation of Feuerbachian "contemplative materialism" via Lyell, Darwin and the stage theory developed by Enlightenment tradition and English political economists. It seeks to explain the reciprocal determination of social being and social consciousness within the totality of historically specific modes of production, revealing a dynamic interrelation where social existence primarily determines consciousness, yet consciousness, in turn, acts upon and transforms concrete social being. The result is a dialectical interplay between being and consciousness, or humanity in nature and nature in humanity (Marx, 1844).
Marxist scientific analysis of social development explains societal evolution through the prism of “modes of production” – a term that encompasses, productive forces, relations of production and superstructural apparatuses. These modes of production are themselves understood to be subject to dialectical laws of development, containing within them via Marx's theory of class struggle (picked up partly from Augustin Thierry) (Marx & Engels, 1854, p.87) the seeds of their own sublation and transcendence. Capitalism's fundamental contradiction is revealed in the tension between inherited property regimes, class antagonism, productive forces and the socio-cultural and political regimes of distribution of surplus. This manifests chiefly in the antagonism between proletariat and bourgeoisie but also in relations of production and advancing techno-scientific capacity. These antagonisms grow ever more acute as capitalism develops, hastening, Marx argued, its eventual demise.
Marx sought to bring down the intellectual handmaidens of capital. For him, the works of Smith, Ricardo, J.S. Mill and N. W. Senior served to obscure the historical relations of production, cloaking exploitation in the garb of “free exchange” and “free movement of labour” (Marx & Engels, 1982, pp. 125–137). According to Marx's labor theory of value, a commodity's value is determined by the socially necessary labor time required for production by humans who, dispossessed of the means of production, sell themselves in piece-bits. This remains true despite the dialectical contradiction between use value and exchange value. For Marx, extraction of surplus value (S) occurs when capital (M), through the purchase of labour-power and ownership of means of production, allows the appropriation of commodity (C) production (Marx & Engels, 1982, pp.125-137). Yet whilst value creation occurs in the commodification of labour-power in the production process (1982, pp.287-320), the circuit of capital is only realized once it meets circulation, trade exchange and consumption (Marx & Engels, 1992). This secondary process (circulation) completes the first (production) (Marx & Engels, 1959, pp.320-333). The cycle now begets more capital (M′). The M-C-M′ circuit exposes how capital, born of the exploitation of past (dead) and living-labor, comes to dominate the working masses. Accordingly, labour's productive power is presented as a force that blossoms in the presence of the powerful and creative will or the “directing authority” of the capitalist (Marx & Engels, 1982, p.231). This domination, a form of economic enslavement, alienates workers from their labor, their production, their social relations, and ultimately, their very essence as a species-being [Gattungswesen] (Marx & Engels, 2000).
The dialectical conception of history, property regimes and productive forces contains within it an explicit theory of technology and an implicit depiction of innovation (which we outline later). For Marx, history unfolds through the interplay of contradictions within the three pillars of his schema: the configuration of the productive forces; the modality of social and politico-economic distribution of surplus; and the composition of class struggle between the property-owning, exploiting, repressive ruling class and the non-property-owning, exploited, oppressed class. As Graeme Kirkpatrick observes, “if human history is thought of as a structure or a building, technology [for Marx] is the foundational level that supports the rest …. [Productive forces have] “an in-built tendency to develop and become more efficient, so that human beings can produce more useable goods from fewer inputs, measured in terms of expended labour and raw materials used” (Kirkpatrick, 2017, p.45). Whereas Rousseau (and many contemporary degrowth scholars) believe surplus necessarily leads to exploitation and catastrophe, Marx's formula sought to allow development of productive forces and accumulation of surplus without exploitation and hierarchy (Marx, 1844; Marx & Engels, 2012). Marxist responses to contemporary ecological crises must draw from this holistic understanding of the simultaneously destructive and productive potential of technology.

4. A Marxist critique of mainstream ecomodernism

A Marxist critique of mainstream ecomodernism begins with the genealogy of Marxist discourse and its regime of thought. Marxism emerges from: the dialectical synthesis of revolutionary Enlightenment ideas and discourses; the historical context of the first and second industrial revolution and the radical expansion of the productive forces; and humanity's revolutionary experiences from the American Declaration of Independence, the French Revolution and the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen, to the Haitian abolition of slavery and the Paris Commune (1871). The Marxist politico-ethical formula envisions an organic social system devoid of the pathologies of “civilization” tied to private property, exploitative features of “industrial societies”, or of the repressive “legacies of hierarchy” (Greene & Fluss, 2020). Marxists put forward a set of foundational axioms: harmony and consociation between nature, biotic life and conscious homo sapiens (Marx & Engels, 1982, p.283); unity between the individual and community; respect for the singularity and difference of diverse peoples (universalism); the eradication of private-property ownership; polymorphous labour; and horizontal organisation of the social body at local (direct participatory democracy) and international level (anti-imperialist confederationalism) (Badiou, 2010).
The political task of materialist revolutionaries was to not only revendicate “early communism” but to birth it in a sublated modern, technologized, internationalized, and cosmopolitan form. Marxism presents a radical account of the future divorced from class struggle, state and technocratic power. The principles of “from each according to his ability, to each according to his needs”, “proletarian democracy”, collective ownership of the means of production, and the “abolition of private property" relations would characterize this new social formation which was intended to free humanity from the contradictions of capital and alienated labor, and from the burdens of repression, discrimination and prejudice (Marx and Engels, 2012).
Ecomodernism's genealogy also lies somewhat in enlightenment. Nevertheless, a Marxist might be expected to critique Breakthrough Institute style ecomodernism for its apparent veneration of a science divorced from and levitating above capitalist social relations, its crude empiricism bereft of dialectical understanding, its faith in instrumental rationalism, its technocratic pragmatic elitism, and its tolerance of hierarchies from state to class (Asafu-Adjaye et al., 2015). These characteristics reveal Ecomodernism as aligned with what Jonathan Israel terms the “moderate enlightenment” (Israel, 2006, pp. 159-162). This moderate camp encompassed thinkers like Hobbes, Locke, Voltaire, Montesquieu, Hume and Smith who sought not the overthrow of the old regime's beliefs and hierarchies, but their preservation with a veneer of rationality and a vertical-modernity. They attempted a grand reconciliation: between modernity and the cesspools of old class social artefacts, between the advances of science and the existing apparatuses of state power, between the new excess political configurations and the entrenched social systems of power. In the moderate enlightenment schema, reason was not the universal birthright of humanity, but a privileged possession of the ruling classes to be wielded for the benefit of the dumb “minors” and “domestic livestock”, as Kant famously put it (Kant, 1996).
Today, mainstream ecomodernists speak of progress and innovation while offering no substantial critique of existing power and capital. They offer technological panaceas for the contradictions of capital, while overlooking the fundamental class antagonisms that are implicated in ecological crises. From a Marxist perspective, mainstream ecomodernist solutions seek to ameliorate the symptoms while leaving the disease of capitalist accumulation untreated. Just as the political and techno-scientific bourgeois revolutions (e.g. American Revolution, industrial revolution) stopped short of true emancipation, so too does ecomodernism's Promethean vision falter long before even considering revolutionary social transformation. It denies that the environmental question is, at its core, a socio-political as well as a productive force question derived from the existing mode of production and its corollary class pathologies. The crisis of nature might theoretically be resolved via development of technologies so advanced they make no demand on nature. However, in the here and now, ecological harm is inseparable from the crisis of capitalist relations of production and its class configurations. Any response that purports to transform the technological basis of production while ignoring the division of property, labour and nations, will at best achieve temporary, reactionary and uneven solutions that will inevitably be overwhelmed by the social contradictions it has failed to resolve.
In the Marxist view, the consequences of ecomodernism's failures are numerous: a misunderstanding of what constitutes an ontology of technology and technique, misapprehensions about what constitutes the aim of the bourgeois state, a naive hope that egalitarian technocratic solutions can be implemented by capitalist states under the greedy and watchful gaze of the bourgeois class, and, notwithstanding ecomodernists' critique of the green face of imperialism (Juma, 2016; Lloyd, 2023; Lynas, 2018), no critique of wider forms of dependency and imperialist incursions. Even ecomodernism's technical policy work rarely engages with what Marxists consider to be the causes of uneven development (unequal exchange of value, super-exploitation, sub-imperialism, intellectual property regimes, etc.). For example, despite their preoccupation with state-backed innovation, ecomodernist scholars have scarcely engaged with how the neo-colonial character of intellectual property law inhibits the spread of clean technologies (Abrol, 2018).
From a Marxist perspective, mainstream ecomodernists fail to grasp how the development of technology and scientific discourse is currently confined within the law of capitalist accumulation, and that this law will undermine policy-interventions promoting innovation. Marx in Capital (Vol I) argues that capitalism's profit imperative is the dialectical co-dependency between “technical composition” (physical ratio of machines to workers in the means of production) and “value composition” (ratio of constant capital to variable capital). The “organic composition of capital”, reflecting both its “technical composition” and “value composition” (Marx & Engels, 1982, p.608), tends to rise as capitalists seek to maximize surplus value and minimize variable capital. Capital's pursuit of profit necessitates a disregard for the extraction and destruction of nature (objectification and commodification of nature to raw material or objects of labour) and aims to minimize necessary labor time relative to surplus labor time. As the organic composition increases, capitalists manipulate variable capital to extract both absolute and relative surplus value. The process simultaneously expands the socio-ecological rift and creates a “reserve army” of labor (Marx & Engels, 1982, p.608). In Vol 1 of Capital Marx argued that with every apparent advance in capitalist productive forces and new cycle of capital accumulation, the rift between social and natural “metabolism” organically expands (Marx and Engels, 1982, pp.431-447).
These cycles lead to the establishment of uneven tiers of development within global capitalism. The mainstream ecomodernists’ synchronic and analytical formula fails here too. In their technocratic quest for “universal human dignity on a biodiverse and thriving planet” (Asafu-Adjaye et al., 2015, p.31) ecomodernists fail to recognise that within the planetary logic of capitalist accumulation development and underdevelopment are not antagonistic but connected, forming a dialectical unity of opposites. The three waves of dependency theorists (Gunder-Frank, Marini, Amin, Wallerstein) are contested figures within the Marxist tradition (see Smith, 2002). However, their arguments concerning unequal structural transfer of value from periphery to core, the super-exploitation of labour in the former colonies (underpayment below reproduction value, extended workdays, and labor intensification) (Marini, 2023), and denial of technological advances to the South warrant careful consideration within assessments of the potential for technological innovation (See Juma, 2016).
Further, environmental degradation in peripheral countries has routinely subsidized production, consumption and accumulation in core countries, highlighting the differentiation of the global working class along gendered, racial, and national lines (Amin, 1976; Marini, 2023; Wallerstein, 1974). Capitalism has routinely created “ecological enclaves”, where local ecosystems and communities in the periphery bear the costs of global production and consumption. Thus, the benefits of technological advances have primarily accrued to the centre; the periphery, denied the advancement of its productive forces, continues to shoulder the burden of environmental degradation and resource depletion, perpetuating historical patterns of colonization and imperialism in a new, technologically mediated form.
Ecomodernism's lack of a clear scientific, dialectical, and materialist formula leads to a technological framework and a political program of action that a Marxist will view as inconsistent and contradictory. Mainstream ecomodernists lack a concrete ontology of techne and science that is holistic and tied to the evolutionary dialectics of nature and social relations. Marxists view the technological life-cycle as an inseparable node in the capitalist regime of property, connected to its technical relations of production, exploitation, and labor division, profit-directed means of production, local and state structures of accumulation, undemocratic hierarchical social systems and irrational ideological systems, and imperialist reorganisation of the international system via enlisting comprador classes. Mainstream ecomodernists' rejection of this view leads not only to reactionary political prescriptions (e.g. acceptance of capitalism, and neglect of the centrality of class-inequality and imperial systems) but to outright epistemological and technical failure. Nevertheless, it is mainstream ecomodernists who have most fully developed an account of innovation's role (e.g. Shellenberg & Nordhaus, 2015); we speculate that more radical scholars' expectation that class struggle can address ecological crises may predispose them to discount the value of accompanying technological transformation. Consequently, Marxist ecological responses could benefit from engaging with mainstream ecomodernism's account of mission-oriented innovation.

5. Toward Marxist axioms of technology and innovation

For Marx, technology is ontologically intertwined with nature, the reproduction of the species, economic processes, social relations and culture. Rooted in dialectical and historical materialism and synthesizing insights from natural and social sciences, Marxist doctrine's epistemic framework, methodology, and weltanschauung provide a comprehensive framework for understanding technology. From this foundation, we can distil a set of axioms – anthropologic-philosophical dialectical statements in the Marxist tradition – that systematically and chronologically elucidate this conception of technology.
  • i.
    Technology, à la Darwin, emerges as an extension of nature and biophysiological organs, dialectically intertwining natural processes of physics, chemistry, and biological evolution with the means of securing sustenance, enhancing bodily capabilities, enabling action and developing instruments of labor.
Technology as the primordial interface is an “anthropological universal” that involves “exteriorization of memory”, “crystallization of body gestures” and the “liberation of the organs” for satisfying needs (Hui, 2017). Early technological development in the palaeolithic age initiates biophysiological transmutations (development of hands, bipedal locomotion, pelvic widening, birth of language and gestures, stone flaking, etc.) and hominid self-creation and development wherein the embryo of a socio-technical organization arises (Engels, 1925). First and second nature (biologically innate and cultural characteristics), are thus interconnected, continuously reshaping both existence and labour to advance the means of subsistence.
  • ii.
    Technology mediates and is an active force that organizes social relations and exchange, shaping the dialectical interaction between human activity and nature. As simple tools extend human biophysiological capacities, they simultaneously transform both the “social metabolism” of human collectives and the broader “universal metabolism” of nature itself (Grundmann, 1991, pp.109-110).
The Mesolithic and neolithic period sees the creation of concrete evolutionary feedback loops where each socio-technological innovation and subsequent advancement in production fundamentally alters the material conditions of human existence.
  • iii.
    Technology, in the form of early simple tools, artefacts or appropriated labour (slaves, subjugated kin, workers, etc.) is the historical force and material basis of evolving production.
Technology evolves and advances dialectically with collective labour and knowledge, and communal adaptation to natural conditions that emerged from the nature-culture continuum. During the Neolithic period, preliterate societies began forming more complex social structures as the division of labour became increasingly sophisticated, enabling specialized roles in horticulture, gardening, pottery, and toolmaking (McClellan III & Dorn, 2006, pp.17-35). These emerging practices of cultivation and craft specialization marked a decisive shift from immediate subsistence.
  • iv.
    The trajectory of technological development (from i. to iii.) in its innate entanglement with social relations of production, produces complex and transitional socio-technical systems
This evolution, through collective human praxis, quantitatively advances techniques from immediate natural processes and tools. In the process it enhances humanity's capacity to transform both itself and nature. These emergent tools and “invisible technologies” (Ede, 2022, p.76) – from social acts (ceremonies, dance, drama, and music) to morality, ideological and philosophical conceptions – are as crucial as tangible tools (horseshoe, sewing needle, fishing net, axe, adze, hammer, plough, bronze blade, chariot, quern, Kiln, etc.) and literate societies' systems (military, writing, accounting, mathematics, etc.) (McClellan III & Dorn, 2006, pp.19-55). Collectively, they lay the groundwork for diverse forms of social organization and divisions of labour. With growth of productive forces, scientific innovations, labor division, techniques and specialization, technology changes the mode of production.
  • v.
    Private property emerges as productive forces develop, generating surplus beyond subsistence needs. This surplus enables wealth accumulation which fosters differentiation of social roles and consolidates resources by elites. Technological ownership becomes a means of asserting social control.
Gradually, differential and unequal access to resources (land, tools, technique, access to cultural and political power) entrenches social stratification – slave and agricultural hierarchical societies exemplify this order. These dynamics first crystallized in early hydraulic civilizations where river valley societies developed complex irrigation networks, administrative bureaucracies, and monumental architecture that demonstrated elite control over collective labor. From Mesopotamian city-states to Greek poleis, these societies established patterns of territorial control, tribute extraction, and technological monopolization that became templates for subsequent state formations (Childe, 1951). Gerontocratic, patriarchal and economic ruling pockets with greater resources structure social institutions. They create institutions and mechanisms that protect their interests, establishing exclusive resource rights and justifying domination of other humans and nature – the foundation of private property. This consolidation accelerates wealth concentration, creating a self-reinforcing cycle where control of resources and technologies leads to further accumulation.
  • vi.
    The “first great cleavage of society into two classes” arises between the resource and technology-controlling exploiters and the labor-selling, technology using exploited (Engels, 2004).
As private property becomes established across expanding territories, labor division intensifies beyond mere specialization, with certain social categories and groups (racial, ethnic, gendered, etc.) gaining control over others' labor. The state evolves from a coordinating mechanism into a specialized instrument of class domination, developing sophisticated legal codes, military institutions, and ideological apparatuses to maintain elite control. To maintain and legitimize this unequal distribution, social institutions and governance structures emerge, including expansion of superstructural mechanisms justifying the new infrastructural order and class society. A technocratic state apparatus forms to protect this new socio-cultural and economic arrangement, solidifying its monopoly over wealth, invisible and material technology, and power (i.e. landlords’ ownership of watermills, road networks, aqueducts, tax and census systems, kayaks, mechanical clocks, stirrups, printing presses, etc.) (Headrick, 2009, pp.15-70) across imperial systems spanning from Rome through the Abbasid and other Islamic empires to the Ottomans. This state-property-technology complex manifested in chartered companies and early corporations, formed under medieval to early modern imperial or feudal patronage to manage globalizing navigation, trade and colonial administration.
  • vii.
    A globalizing structure of accumulation (enclosures, industrialization, technocracy, colonial processes, etc.) subjugates and integrates the tricontinent, with its distinct geopolitical circuits, devouring its society and biosphere as a node within the international machinery of extraction and exploitation. The global domination of capital materializes as a technological regime.
Technology becomes the primary motive force in society and nature subject to constant renewal and updating (due to material deterioration or “moral depreciation”) (Marx & Engels, 1982, pp.259-302). Technological systems hide the social history of class relations and how they emerged. With time these systems come to appear almost metaphysical or too complex for workers to own, access, or comprehend (Marx & Engels, 1982). From 1492 onward, European ruling class expansion integrated the tricontinent into the globalizing capitalist system, imposing a regime of technology (consisting of gunpowder artillery, transoceanic ships like caravels and galleons, the astrolabe, printing press, sugar mills, and double-entry bookkeeping, firearms manufacturing, etc.), unequal value exchange and super-exploitation that subordinated its societies within the global divide.
  • viii.
    The socioeconomic structures of class societies shaped the emergence and development of Enlightenment thought, scientific rationalism and technological imaginaries, imbuing them with the interests and ideological perspectives of the ascendant imperialist bourgeoisie.
With capitalist modernity and the Industrial Revolution technology becomes further technocratized, geopoliticized, scientificized and gendered. This shift marks the transition from “formal” to “real subsumption” of labor under capital, fundamentally reshaping the production process itself. Mechanization systematically deskills traditional craftwork, replacing artisanal knowledge with machine processes controlled by capital. Complex innovations (e.g. advanced metallurgy, steam engine, telegraph, locomotive, ships, transatlantic travel, electricity, etc.) require vast capital and highly specialized scientific labor, placing their production beyond the comprehension and control of ordinary workers and society. Industrial technology reinforces gender divisions while accelerated production cycles unleash planetary-scale environmental transformation (Ede, 2022, pp. 230-250). Consequently, the new age's scientific, technological and cultural achievements (the “great significance of industry”, Marx & Engels, 2022, p.165) rarely benefit workers but instead intensify class and colonial divisions and worker alienation.
  • ix.
    Just as technique and technology can be mechanisms of “extortion and despotism” so they can become mechanisms of new social formations, conditions of labour and production and the embryo of a new revolutionary egalitarian modern society.
Technology, technique, and science give birth to their own possibilities of mobilization, resistance, and revolution. Industrial capitalism concentrated workers and subjected them to the mechanical power of steam-driven factory systems. It imposed technological domination through evolving management techniques. These evolved from early industrial discipline through electrified Fordism-Taylorism and assembly lines to just-in-time production and digitalised neoliberal methods employing algorithmic surveillance and data extraction. These conditions sparked resistance movements from Luddites to Chartists, the Internationals, communist parties, and modern labour organizing. These movements contested wages, working conditions, and crucially, who owns and controls technology and its purposes. Largely overlooked by Marxist theorists, the struggle over technology's identity and ontological position in class relations became central to revolutionary praxis. These material conditions then allow us to state that Marxism emerges as a technology of resistance and revolution forged by capitalism's first colonized subjects, the European working classes, and later indigenous and colonized peoples whose labor and lands were violently seized for the accumulation process.
  • x.
    Technology must be socialized by the dictatorship of the proletariat.
For Marx, technological innovation constantly outpaces the social world and relations of production; as such, it is both a source of tension and celebration. In fact, the dialectical tension located in class societies, ensures that key to Marx's theory of emancipation and revolution is the perpetual oscillation of forces, like Newton's cradle where energy transfers unevenly through colliding registers, creating dynamic disequilibrium between innovation, crisis and contradictions.
Marxism is the innovation of a politics of the wretched universal class that aims to free the productive forces from the exploitation of the property-owning class and the finitudes of nature. The question is then why, although this push for a substantial materialist position on innovation is implicit in Marx's politics, Marx offers no explicit directive on innovation? Why have Marxists not made it central to their history, slogans and revolutionary objectives?
Two historical instances present a clarification. The first is Marx's response to Luddism, which he viewed as the worker's expression of their alienation and estrangement[Entfremdung] from their species-being under capitalist conditions (Marx & Engels, 2000). Marx, in Capital's seminal chapter on technology, writes that “with the advent of machinery … the workman for the first time brutally revolts against the instruments of labour. The instrument of labour strikes down the labourer” (Marx & Engels, 1982, p.288). He laments this antagonism. Only in mechanized factories do workers first systematically understand the connections between technology, innovation, exploitation and revolution. Marx's dialectical position is that while technology isn't responsible for the violent "imposition of the political economy of laissez-faire upon, and against the will and conscience of, the working people" (Thompson in Webber, 2015), the labour movement must critically appropriate scientific advancements – including ruling-class property. This stand of preserving and re-orienting existing tools, machinery and “systems of machines” was perceived as central for the historical process where the working class first identifies its foe and shifts from a “class-in-itself" to a “class-for-itself".
For the second historical instance we can take two seminal positions from Lenin and Mao. First, Lenin's famous slogan that “Communism is Soviet power plus the electrification of the whole country’ (Lenin, 1920a,b). For Lenin and the Bolsheviks, the shift from the “realm of necessity” to “realm of freedom” (communism) is made possible by emancipating the productive forces and intensifying advances in technology and science. Mao, in turn, asserted that in development and innovation one should not “crawl step by step behind” capitalists that have “a high scientific and technological level [and] are overblown with arrogance” (Mao, 1956). Advocating for development of productive forces and innovation in the former colonies, Mao wrote that Communism must “break the routine and adopt as many innovative techniques as possible in order to build our country into a powerful modern socialist state” (Mao, 1956). For both Lenin and Mao, the pathway to communism is through abolishing private property, establishing radical democratic egalitarian communes and seizing and expediting techno-scientific innovation.
Two instances, two technical dilemmas. Marx's response to Luddites, and Lenin and Mao's position each seek to preserve existing technological advances and emphasise the value of further innovation to emancipation. The Luddite movement and Marx's critique of it occurs within and during the end of the first communist sequence–beginning from the French revolution to the Paris commune. Lenin and Mao's advocacy for innovation arose with communists in power in the second sequence that began in 1917 and ended in 1976 with the death of Mao (Badiou, 2010).
Add the collapse of real socialist states and communist movements after the 1990s and we have our answer why innovation – outside the Chinese Communist Party – has not been central to Marxist accounts of technology or responses to ecological crises. Social consciousness is inexorably tethered to and conditioned by the evolving contradictions of the relations of production and property regime. The two cases above reflect a belated recognition and articulation of already-extant material transformations. In the words of Hegel, the “rational is actual and the actual is rational” (Hegel, 1991, p.20). Empirico-historically, this means that the concept of innovation is only formulated as a policy when the communist movement is directing the productive forces and in charge of the state-property machinery–albeit always under imperial duress. Retroactively we can also grasp why Marx's conception of innovation lacks a concrete axiom and directive. Limited and anchored to the historical material circumstances of labour-movements and the advances of capital and productive forces, Marx remains tied to the idea that innovation must be taken out of the hands of the bourgeois capitalist class, and its future trajectory will be determined by the communist movement in the post-capitalist world.
Marx's position on technology responded to the miracles of the industrial revolution and the problem of capitalist exploitation. By contrast, debate between socialist-ecomodernist and degrowth scholars responds to a set of contemporary problems like climate change and ocean acidification that were unknown in the nineteenth century. These global ecological problems a) can only be identified and understood through highly specialized and applied scientific research, and b) emerge primarily as unintended consequences from the aggregation of ostensibly harmless actions (e.g. electricity generation, constructing homes and workplaces, transport, industrial smelting). Of course, harmful activities like military operations and gas flaring also play a role. To address these indirect ecological consequences, societies must either radically scale down the technologies that meet human material needs, or deliberately transform productive forces to completely decouple human development from ecological impacts.
Ecomodernism's central contribution has been to reject the Heideggerian and Ellulian critique of technology that shaped the green movement, and to identify “mission-oriented” technological transformation as foundational within any effective response to global ecological challenges. By recognising that fossil fuels are both destroying the conditions of planetary reproduction and are also still fundamental to meeting human material needs, ecomodernism highlights the idealism or hypocrite of those who deny this dual reality by claiming elite class interests are the only barrier to ending fossil fuel use. However, Ecomodernism's central failure is to imagine that technological transformation can be accomplished by scientised state-capitalist technocratic apparatuses that are inherently exploitative and imperial.
Since carbon dioxide is a stock pollutant, global temperatures will only stabilise when greenhouse gas emissions reach “net-zero” (MacDougall et al., 2020). Net-zero requires complete elimination, not just degrowth, of all polluting forms of production. Difficult-to-decarbonise sectors must either be eliminated completely, or alternative technologies developed. Restoring pre-industrial atmospheric conditions would require a multi-decade, energy-intensive project of carbon drawdown. These goals would demand equitable, universal deployment of clean electricity generation; deployment of replacements for emission-intensive technologies in construction, shipping, aviation, transport, industrial processes, heating, and food production; and gigatonne-scale deployment of negative emissions technologies. While some technologies exist (e.g. zero-carbon electricity), the workforce and infrastructure needed for equitable global deployment do not. Training a global workforce capable of remediating the Capitalocene's ecological destruction while curbing capitalist harm to the social sphere will be part of the work of any future communist movement and society. This is why Marxist doctrine must be enriched via a novel axiom that systematises and popularises seizing the means of innovation.
Recent capitulation of Western socialism to the degrowth movement has de-emphasised this long communist tradition and with it the question of technological advances (see Hickel et al., 2022; Saito, 2023). By advocating for a socialism that fails to centre technological modernisation, this movement risks promulgating a socialism of toil and a revolution of sacrifice. By emphasising the centrality of surplus extraction and growth via advancements in productive forces as the cause of ecological crisis, it offers no satisfactory answers to the challenges of ecology, labor or subsistence. Or worse, it risks surrendering the force of technological progress to hierarchical and authoritarian reactionary movements (Nanda, 2003). As Huber and Phillips (2024) have pointed out, this is both catastrophic and unnecessary. It is catastrophic because degrowth appears to offer a “politics of less” for future societies and the working masses who already have very little. And unnecessary because simultaneous advances in social, scientific and technological provisioning would allow further progress toward both ecological restoration and human welfare.

6. Afterthought

The fundamental question of technology is not just one of technique and science, hence a technocratic and bureaucratic problem (as it might be perceived by mainstream ecomodernists), nor is it just the economic question, one in which productive forces are dictated by a form of ownership that extracts from nature and exploits labor for capital accumulation (so called Marxist economism). Technology does pose technical and economic problems. But by amalgamating these with Marx's philosophico-anthropological and revolutionary formula distilled in our axioms, we can identify technology's politico-ontological implications. A Marxist position on technology requires organizing social relations, civil society, the property dilemma, the state, and its relationship with nature in a harmonious formula. In other words, the techno-logic question is linked to old foundational questions about the ontological structure of the state, the relationship between order and equality, as well as of political subjectivity in structures and nature (Hui, 2016). These are lines of inquiry tackled long ago not only by Marx, but also Plato, Aristotle, Hobbes and Hegel. Peter Thiel's boast that “technology is an alternative for politics” (cited in Von Knebel, 2025) suggests this is well understood by some of those who most embody the contradictions of late-stage capitalism.
In this light, we must conceive the technological innovation question today, like the question Plato or Marx find in the demos or proletariat, as the problem of the ontological count of the system – a phenomenon which escapes and outruns the capitalist system. The social does not equate with the technological; they are qualitatively unequal, but both, for different reasons, play the part that reconfigure all parts. In this sense, both questions of social multiple and technology as an active-multiple (an independent and potentially sentient force that has its own cosmopolitical inertia) concern what social formations (state-capital) do with excess and their supernumerary corollary effect. Both have ontological, existential and political implications for social and natural metabolisms. Thiel is aware of this too. He declared in 2024 “you could unilaterally change the world without having to constantly convince people and beg people and plead with people who are never going to agree with you …”. How? “… through technological means” (Von Knebel, 2025).
It is not just the uncounted and supernumerary proletariat and their “empty double freedom” that create the “fundamental dilemma” of politics and social relations (Rancière, 1999). It is also the geometric disarray which technique and technology impose on the social formation (e.g. labour organisation, social integration, distribution of surplus) polis, and nature. The Anthropocene question then is a “cosmotechnical” issue since modernity has reduced the earth, society and cosmos into a gigantic technological system (Hui, 2017). Marx recognizes this dialectical entanglement; for him, each cycle of realization of capital, intensification of exploitation, revolution, or innovation reshapes issues of equality, justice, distribution, and metabolism with nature. As such, social revolt and technology stem from contradictions, dynamism, fluidity, and excess. But for Marx they also require socio-technological and subjective vigilance – they are both active-multiples of some kind. This connects to Latour's argument, outlined in a Breakthrough Institute paper, that “unexpected consequences are attached to their initiators and have to be followed through all the way” (Latour, 2011).
The outcomes of innovation – whether promoting or undermining equality and progress –depend on political subjectivity and the ownership, control, design and distribution of new technologies. Science must be shackled to egalitarianism – “Prometheus plus Spartacus” in Hal Draper's slogan (1971). This formula is abstractly or qualitatively correct. However, the ecomodernists' detailed study of technology in the context of climate change presents quantitative premises to Marxism's grand socio-technological revolutionary system. Avoiding climate and ecological crisis now requires some combination of social and technological “miracles” (Kallis, 2019, p. 195) to ease human pressure on natural systems. Capitalist contradictions have brought us to a critical juncture with a clear path: catastropheor communism.
The pathway for communism thus requires Marxists to concede a new premise: that the socio-technological formula of the first wave of communism (1789–1871) which anticipates the proletariat can liberate and advance the productive forces by default through revolution, requires more concrete conceptualization today. Marx attempts to close this technical innovation and scientific gap via a wager: confidence that the subject of history, the proletariat will be up to the task. Sure, but contemporary polycrisis imposes a temporal and political constraint that requires more than a poetic self-authoring wager on the “last” or “universal” class.
It requires Marxists to contend with the fact that this universal class is internally split –across historical, sociological, geopolitical, and technical lines (Marx, 1844). The formation of global capital has ensured that this class is topologically uneven, spanning core and periphery, mental and manual labour, skilled and unskilled strata. These divisions structure contradictions and antagonisms within the international division of labour and humanity. Here, the social force engaged in specialized intellectual labour – the "innovatariat" (e.g. engineers, coders, climatologists, epidemiologists) – occupies a structurally ambivalent position.1 Their technical composition (embedded in high-skill production, and proximate to capitalist life-cycle of technological innovation) and political composition (alignment with states, managerialist ideology and technocratic rationality) ensures a dissymmetry vis-à-vis the broader global proletariat (Pitts, 2024). This stratification fractures the subjective topology of the innovatariat, producing divergences in material needs, political orientation, ecological consciousness, techno-scientific proximity, and revolutionary potential. The challenge of Marxism, through its organizational praxis, is to methodologically hold together this polarizing unequal terrain. Not by collapsing difference into sameness, but by sustaining the dialectical unity of opposites and differences.
This way the universal class can allow for a revolutionary recomposition that forges a collective subject capable of abolition, transition and recreation of the productive forces. Concretely this means aligning the scientific and technical capacities of specialized workers with the mass of humanity's struggle for ecological justice and material emancipation. This raises foundational practical questions. In what ways can universal class consciousness be cultivated? And how can innovation, under proletarian command, be reconstituted as a tool for metabolic restoration and class recomposition?
Transforming innovation into an instrument of planetary repair requires strategic recomposition of social forces under the leadership of this universal class. Many of the most immediately feasible steps align with ecomodernists and Southern states’ agendas – defending existing national institutions (e.g. universities & science agencies); and supporting reconfiguration of international climate, energy, intellectual property and innovation governance structures (e.g. IPCC, International Energy Agency, TRIPS, Mission Innovation) to support the global proletariat rather than Western power. However, moving beyond this reformist agenda requires building revolutionary unions and party organizations capable of opposing capital and intervening across the full life cycle of commodified technologies. The stages of invention, growth, acceptance, standardization could then become moments where the technical class moves cumulatively from an in-itself to for-itself class force. Brought under democratic control, techno-scientific innovation would seek to advance the material needs of the wretched and ecological health.
Specialized labour should be reorganized into politicized technical cadres (cells) within these institutions functioning in cohesion with the wider unions and production sites.2 Once engaged with the material needs of the majority of humanity, these technical cells might shift the PMC from being a handmaiden of capitalist production to a position of active authorship of socially embedded, ecologically rational systems – these technical cells could facilitate what Ursula Franklin calls a shift from 'prescriptive' to holistic technologies, guiding productive processes away from top-down control (Franklin, 1990). Rather than treating innovation as a neutral domain, this process reclaims it as a site of class and anti-imperial struggle, opening it to collective design and centralized democratic planning. This quantitative step moves toward the qualitative goal of seizing both production and innovation. This is where the “universal class” can via the “dictatorship of the proletariat”, through state institutions, dismantle capitalist technological order and construct a communist horizon in which technical reason serves collective emancipation, not accumulation.
This leads us dialectically back to an eleventh axiom (or thesis). Marxism today requires a sublation of that old Maoist principle, “no communism without a communist movement” (Badiou, 2018). To this we may add, “and communist technological innovation”. It is in this light that we put forward the necessity for a communist axiom of innovation.
  • Xi.
    The Communist goal of seizing the means of production contains implications concerning technology that should now be made explicit; the task is also to seize the means of innovation.

1
We thank an anonymous reviewer for prompting this discussion about cross-class solidarity and innovation and suggesting the term "innovatariat".
2
Historical examples include: Soviet collaborative development of major economic textbooks involving hundreds of economists with worker and managerial input; Lenin's GOELRO electrification program from 1920 mobilizing over 200 scientific and technical experts alongside peasants and workers, with Lenin declaring that not politicians, administrators and bureaucrats but “engineers and agronomists [representing the people's direct needs] will do most of the talking” (Lenin, 1920, p. 15) at Soviet congresses; contemporary Chinese deployment of agricultural scientists to rural communities for technical assistance in sustainable farming practices and poverty alleviation.
